articles.title: Why the Government should rethink the emergency visa brake on study visas
articles.summary: The UK Government recently announced an ‘emergency visa brake’ on study visas for university applicants from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan. This policy was critiqued in a blog post authored by Sasha Roseneil, Vice-Chancellor and President of the University of Sussex, and published by HEPI.
articles.insight:
This UK policy signals potential shifts in student demand from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan, possibly increasing interest in alternative destinations like the US, Australia, and New Zealand—though actual spillover depends on regional awareness, eligibility, and competing visa frameworks.  
Admissions teams should monitor application trends from these four countries and adjacent regions (e.g., West Africa, Southeast Asia), while reviewing their outreach messaging, scholarship offerings, and visa support services to ensure responsiveness to evolving geopolitical mobility constraints.

articles.title: A policy for allowing foreign universities setting up Indian campuses
articles.summary: The Indian government approved a policy in 2023 permitting top-ranked foreign universities to establish campuses in India under specific conditions, including a minimum global ranking threshold and adherence to Indian regulatory frameworks. These campuses must operate as not-for-profit entities, offer programs aligned with India’s higher education standards, and obtain prior approval from the University Grants Commission and other relevant authorities.
articles.insight:
This policy may gradually reshape demand for outbound Indian students by offering high-quality, globally recognised degrees locally—though its immediate impact on overseas enrolment remains uncertain given limited initial campus approvals and likely modest intake capacity.  
Admissions teams should monitor early implementation data—including which universities launch campuses, their programme offerings, and student uptake—to assess whether this becomes a meaningful alternative to studying abroad over the medium term.

articles.title: Indonesia eyes visa overhaul to boost int’l student numbers
articles.summary: Indonesia is planning to reform its study visa system to increase the number of international students. This initiative responds to growing internationalisation efforts by other ASEAN countries.
articles.insight:
Indonesia’s planned study visa reforms may modestly shift regional student mobility patterns, particularly among Southeast Asian applicants weighing cost, proximity, and regulatory ease—though direct impact on Western university enrolments remains uncertain without evidence of reduced outbound intent.  
Admissions teams should track Indonesia’s implementation timeline, eligibility criteria, and uptake data over the next 12–18 months, while reviewing their Southeast Asian outreach to ensure value propositions remain competitive against emerging regional alternatives.

articles.title: Why are more foreign students choosing India for higher education
articles.summary: India is attracting more foreign students due to its affordable tuition fees, globally recognized degrees, and the growing number of English-taught programs across universities. Government initiatives like the Study in India program and streamlined visa processes have also contributed to the rise in international student enrollment.
articles.insight:
India’s growing appeal to foreign students—driven by affordability, English-taught programs, and supportive policies—suggests potential softening in demand for traditional Anglophone destinations, particularly among cost-sensitive applicants from Asia and Africa, though direct substitution remains unproven.  
Admissions teams should track enrollment trends among students from India’s key source markets (e.g., Nigeria, Nepal, Bangladesh) and assess whether competitors are adjusting pricing, scholarship offerings, or pathway partnerships in response to India’s strengthened positioning.

articles.title: US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand
articles.summary: US student visa issuances fell by 36% in summer 2025, with uncertainty surrounding the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme cited as a key factor affecting international student demand. The US government has renewed its focus on the OPT programme, and former Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem, who was replaced on 5 March, had previously responded favourably to Senator Eric…
articles.insight:
The 36% drop in US student visa issuances during summer 2025—linked partly to OPT uncertainty—suggests growing caution among international applicants, though it remains unclear whether this reflects temporary policy anxiety or a deeper, sustained shift in destination preference across key source markets.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application and enrolment trends by region and programme level, while closely monitoring official US guidance on OPT rule changes, stakeholder sentiment in education fairs, and comparative visa processing timelines in competing destinations.

articles.title: New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students
articles.summary: New Zealand plans to introduce a new Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extend eligibility for the Post Study Work Visa, with implementation scheduled for late 2026. No specific launch date has been confirmed, and the government’s international education strategy emphasizes managed growth over the coming years.
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s planned 2026 visa reforms signal a strategic effort to retain talent post-study, potentially strengthening its appeal—especially among students from Asia and the Pacific—though actual demand shifts depend on implementation details and concurrent policy changes elsewhere.  
Admissions teams should track New Zealand’s final visa criteria, regional marketing responses, and comparative post-study work offerings in Australia, the UK, and Canada, as these will shape student decision-making amid tightening global mobility pathways.

articles.title: Dozens of international students who won Chevening scholarships were denied visas
articles.summary: The UK Home Office denied visa applications for dozens of international students who had been awarded Chevening scholarships from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan. This decision followed a prior ban on student visas for nationals from these countries.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential volatility in UK visa policy toward students from high-risk or conflict-affected regions, possibly dampening demand for UK study among nationals from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan—and indirectly affecting perceptions across broader Southeast Asian and West African markets.  
Admissions teams should monitor UK Home Office guidance updates, track application and refusal trends by nationality, and assess whether affected students are redirecting applications to alternative destinations like the US, Australia, or New Zealand amid shifting policy sentiment.

articles.title: UK denies visas to some winners of Chevening, its own most prestigious scholarship
articles.summary: The UK government has denied student visas to dozens of Chevening Scholarship winners, its most prestigious international scholarship program. This occurred after the UK imposed a blanket ban on student visas from four countries affected by war.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential reputational and trust risks for the UK’s international education brand, possibly dampening demand from conflict-affected regions—even if broader visa policy shifts remain uncertain, it may amplify perceptions of unpredictability in UK immigration pathways.  
Admissions teams should monitor application trends from high-risk source countries, track official visa guidance updates, and assess whether peer institutions are adjusting outreach or support services for students navigating complex geopolitical barriers to study.

articles.title: New Zealand introduces new six-month graduate work visa
articles.summary: The New Zealand government has introduced a new six-month graduate work visa as part of its effort to expand the international education sector. This visa provides a pathway for international graduates to work in New Zealand after completing their studies.
articles.insight:
This policy signals New Zealand’s intent to strengthen its appeal to international students by enhancing post-study work options, potentially influencing student choice—though direct impact on demand for US, UK or Australian institutions remains uncertain amid broader economic and geopolitical factors.  
Admissions teams should monitor enrolment trends among students from key source markets (e.g., India, China, Nepal) applying to NZ institutions, while assessing whether their own post-study work offerings remain competitive in messaging and duration.
